title: First U.S. Small Modular Reactor Achieves Criticality: TRISO Fuel Breakthrough
text:
The Trump Administration issued an executive order in 2020 to accelerate nuclear power development in the U.S., aiming to have three reactor designs reach criticality within 14 months. On Thursday, Antares, a nuclear startup, announced that its test reactor at the Idaho National Laboratory had achieved this milestone, becoming the first new design to do so.
Criticality—where nuclear reactions become self-sustaining—was reached, though the reactor has not yet begun generating power.
Antares’ TRISO-Based Reactor Design
Antares is among several companies pioneering small modular reactors (SMRs) using TRISO fuel, a next-generation nuclear fuel system designed to enhance safety and simplify reactor construction. TRISO fuel consists of tiny uranium oxide pellets encased in multiple layers of carbon and a hard ceramic shell, which can withstand extreme temperatures.
The fuel’s layered structure moderates neutron energy and contains fission products, reducing risks associated with traditional reactor designs. This approach shifts complexity from the reactor hardware to the fuel itself, potentially improving safety and scalability.
Policy Impact and Future Outlook
The executive order aimed to fast-track SMR development, but only one design has been fully licensed to date, with no immediate plans for construction. Antares’ achievement represents a critical step toward commercializing SMRs, though further testing and regulatory approvals will be required before deployment.
While the reactor’s criticality is a technical milestone, it does not yet signal the start of power generation. The focus now shifts to evaluating performance and advancing toward operational readiness.
